title: Year-End Mailbag! The Top 10 Performances, Trailers, and Needle Drops of 2021
format: Sean and Amanda run dueling top-five performance lists to close out 2021, then Bobby Wagner joins for a mailbag on repertory double features, trailer hype, needle drops, and who deserves Best Picture.

films.title: C'mon C'mon
films.note: No. 1 - Joaquin Phoenix as an uncle turned stand-in father, playing off newcomer Woody Norman. "Always nice to have him acting like a human being and not a clown prince of crime."

films.title: The Last Duel
films.note: No. 2 - really a pick for Ben Affleck's whole year (the Snyder Cut, the JLo reunion, the Bill Simmons snub), but his underrated work here, playing a part he co-wrote with self-aware humor about his own celebrity, is the anchor.

films.title: No Time to Die
films.note: No. 3 - Ana de Armas walks away with the movie in a single scene. "She just absolutely blew that scene out of the water."

films.title: Black Widow
films.note: No. 4 - Florence Pugh eating Scarlett Johansson's lunch, then doing it again across Hawkeye.

films.title: The Green Knight
films.note: No. 5 - Dev Patel as a "sad fail son who becomes a knight," holding together a movie that's trying to undermine its own genre.

films.title: The Worst Person in the World
films.note: No. 1 - Renate Reinsve running the emotional gamut of turning 30. Barely seen in the U.S. yet, but the year's best performance nobody can watch.

films.title: Pig
films.note: No. 2 - Nicolas Cage as a man looking for his kidnapped pig. "It doesn't work without him" - credit the restraint and physical bearing, beyond the dinner-scene monologue everyone quotes.

films.title: Zola
films.note: No. 3 - Colman Domingo playing a pimp and hustler in two personas at once. "He does sinister really, really well."

films.title: Red Rocket
films.note: No. 4 - Simon Rex as a washed-up porn star back in his Texas hometown. A charismatic scumbag performance with no easy comparison.

films.title: The Lost Daughter
films.note: No. 5 - Jessie Buckley and Dagmara Domińczyk as, respectively, the young version of Olivia Colman's character and a pregnant beach interloper. Two supporting turns in one slot.

segments.heading: Honorable mentions
segments.note: the ones that didn't make the top five

films.title: The Night House
films.note: Rebecca Hall, all in on an upsetting horror film.

films.title: Passing
films.note: Ruth Negga, directed by Rebecca Hall, trending toward a Best Supporting Actress nomination.

films.title: Dune
films.note: Jason Momoa as Duncan Idaho, the only one who seems to be having fun. Also a nod to Timothée Chalamet for not screwing up Paul Atreides, and to Denis Villeneuve for being right about theaters.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: Alana Haim - "a really well-directed performance," better on a second viewing.

films.title: Parallel Mothers
films.note: Penélope Cruz, a Best Actress possibility even without Spain's submission behind her.

films.title: King Richard
films.note: Will Smith and Aunjanue Ellis, plus a shout to Jon Bernthal in a small role.

films.title: House of Gucci
films.note: Lady Gaga and Jared Leto going full more-is-more.

films.title: tick, tick... BOOM!
films.note: Andrew Garfield's twofer year, part one.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: Andrew Garfield's twofer year, part two - deeply touching work everyone's been texting about.

films.title: West Side Story
films.note: Ariana DeBose, a lock for recognition, and Mike Faist, whom the hosts had heard was quitting acting for van life (he isn't).

films.title: The Suicide Squad
films.note: John Cena - "he really got the assignment."

films.title: Nobody
films.note: Bob Odenkirk, trained and different enough from Saul Goodman to make it work.

films.title: Shiva Baby
films.note: Rachel Sennott, a sweaty, anxious screen style unlike anyone else's.

films.title: Old
films.note: Vicky Krieps, good enough that the hosts want her cast in more mainstream work despite the movie's magic-ending problem.

films.title: Bergman Island
films.note: Vicky Krieps again, in a movie Amanda loved this year.

films.title: Drive My Car
films.note: Hidetoshi Nishijima - "probably the performance of the year that has no chance to be recognized," a restrained director holding back until the movie's center explodes.

films.title: Bo Burnham: Inside
films.note: Bo Burnham, treated as a movie performance even though the hosts flip-flop on whether it counts as one.

films.title: Belfast
films.note: Ciarán Hinds gets the pick over Judi Dench's fourteen Oscar nominations; Jamie Dornan and Caitríona Balfe get a nod for being distractingly attractive parents.

films.title: Mass
films.note: Ann Dowd, the standout of a very upsetting Fran Kranz film about the parents of a school shooter and a victim.

films.title: Those Who Wish Me Dead
films.note: Jon Bernthal, part of a busy year that also included The Many Saints of Newark, Small Engine Repair, and a trailer for The Unforgivable.

films.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
films.note: Tony Leung - "the superpower of that film," no stranger to crime pictures, new to dragons.

segments.heading: Three movies to explain the 2010s to an alien

picks.title: Hillbilly Elegy
picks.note: The unintentional-capture-of-a-terrible-country pick. Sean watched it election night 2020 and has no regrets.

picks.title: The Master
picks.note: Idolatry and false prophets as a defining theme of the decade.

picks.title: Manchester by the Sea
picks.note: "Fuck, what have we done? What is our life?"

picks.title: Get Out
picks.note: Number one for how it captures power, race, and delusion.

picks.title: The Big Short
picks.note: "Everything in the world is the Big Short."

segments.heading: Worst movie moment of the year

picks.title: Dear Evan Hansen
picks.note: The first time anyone starts singing, and every time after.

picks.title: Space Jam: A New Legacy
picks.note: LeBron James sucked into the Warner Bros. multiverse. "It's so painful."

segments.heading: The Big Picture's own repertory theater
segments.note: Drew's question - opening night double (or triple) feature

films.title: Heat
films.note: Chris Ryan's imagined opener, paired with itself and a Den of Thieves chaser.

films.title: Den of Thieves
films.note: The chaser to Chris Ryan's Heat double.

films.title: Moneyball
films.note: Sean and Amanda's actual pick - the movie they've talked about and returned to the most.

films.title: The Social Network
films.note: Paired with Moneyball as most representative of the show.

films.title: Zack Snyder's Justice League
films.note: Bobby's suggested third film. Vetoed.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Bobby's alternate new-beverly-style suggestion.

segments.heading: Zach's birthday rom-com homework
segments.note: picking between Roman Holiday, Broadcast News, and Something's Gotta Give

films.title: Roman Holiday
films.note: Sean's least essential of the three - delightful, but skippable.

films.title: Broadcast News
films.note: Sean's recommendation as a foundation text before the more modern picks.

films.title: Something's Gotta Give
films.note: Amanda's answer - the quintessential Nancy Meyers movie, Diane Keaton, Jack Nicholson, a Hamptons house, and that opening needle drop.

films.title: Down with Love
films.note: Amanda's bonus pick - Peyton Reed's Doris Day riff.

films.title: Sweet Smell of Success
films.note: The one poster currently hanging - Sean's favorite screenplay of all time.

films.title: Network
films.note: Sean's favorite item he owns, never hung: an oversized French poster his wife bought him on their honeymoon.

segments.heading: Trailers that lived up to the hype

films.title: House of Gucci
films.note: "Needed to be two plus hours of the trailer" - and delivered.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: The movie somehow topped an already-hyped trailer.

films.title: Judas and the Black Messiah
films.note: A 2020-trailer, 2021-release title with one of the great trailers of the last five years.

films.title: The Batman
films.note: The best trailer of the year for a movie that hadn't come out yet, set to Nirvana's "Something in the Way."

films.title: Zola
films.note: Self-contained, and it captures the whole film.

films.title: Red Rocket
films.note: Same praise as Zola - a trailer that nails the movie's tone.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: Paul Thomas Anderson cuts his own trailers. "Perfect trailer."

segments.heading: One movie we'd give a do-over

films.title: Spencer
films.note: Sean's pick, for the pun as much as anything - advice: "have somebody else make it."

films.title: In the Heights
films.note: Amanda's pick - wanted it to work, and it mostly does, but it's a little disappointing.

films.title: The Woman in the Window
films.note: Too much talent involved for a thriller that needed a satisfying reveal and didn't have one.

films.title: Beckett
films.note: John David Washington's Netflix thriller - the kind of setup that should've been a Rewatchables staple, minus the follow-through.

segments.heading: Favorite needle drops of the year

films.title: Spencer
films.note: "All I Need Is a Miracle" by Mike + The Mechanics, closing the film.

films.title: The Night House
films.note: "The Cavalry Cross" by Richard and Linda Thompson, a recurring cue signaling the late husband's presence.

films.title: Bergman Island
films.note: ABBA's "The Winner Takes It All," Mia Wasikowska dancing around a fire scene.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: De La Soul's "The Magic Number" over the end credits - a riff on "Three Is a Magic Number" for a movie with three Spider-Men.

films.title: Titane
films.note: "Doing It to Death" and "She's Not There" both feature.

films.title: Red Rocket
films.note: *NSYNC's "Bye Bye Bye," switching from a haunted girls'-chorus cover to the real thing.

films.title: Last Night in Soho
films.note: The original James Ray recording of "I've Got My Mind Set on You," scoring a dance sequence with Matt Smith and Anya Taylor-Joy.

films.title: Judas and the Black Messiah
films.note: The Outlaw Blues Band's "Deep Gully" as Daniel Kaluuya's character enters the club.

films.title: Zola
films.note: The Clickettes' doo-wop "Because of My Best Friend," secretly the film's whole theme.

films.title: Licorice Pizza
films.note: "Let Me Roll It" - the number-one pick, spoiled on the show many times already.

films.title: House of Gucci
films.note: George Michael's "Faith" as Lady Gaga walks down the aisle, straight out of full-on opera.

segments.heading: Best (and worst) Best Picture winner for the industry

films.title: Free Guy
films.note: The hoped-against worst-case winner.

films.title: Eternals
films.note: The other worst-case answer.

films.title: Dune
films.note: The Mad Max: Fury Road comparison - a real visual achievement people actually saw, but still IP winning the top prize.

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: Bobby's pick to win, and a plausible one - Netflix's first potential Best Picture, though back-to-back streaming-era winners (after Nomadland) is a mixed signal for theatrical.

films.title: Belfast
films.note: Part of the late-search conversation alongside Power of the Dog.

films.title: King Richard
films.note: A possible late surge - flatters and subverts the Academy's sensibilities at once.

films.title: Don't Look Up
films.note: The hypothetical none of them would actually cheer for.

films.title: West Side Story
films.note: Amanda's actual worst-case winner - a remake of a musical that already won, that barely anyone saw in theaters.

Ads: Netflix FIFA World Cup Launch Edition

Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Ring.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood
